OMP_NUM_THREADS may be something like "4, 3" to represent top-level and nested OMP thread values.  atoi("4, 3") will return "4", as will dmlc::GetEnv("OMP_NUM_THREADS"), thus nuking (and also incorrectly interpreting) the intended behavior of the set OMP_NUM_THREADS variable.  This is why I explicitly test if the environment variable exists rather than accepting a default, since at that point, I won't know if I am going to override the intended "4, 3" behavior that the user has set.  In  tests since then, I have found that managing both the outer and nested thread counts can affect performance in mxnet.

Using `contextlib.contextmanager` will usually be the simplest way to implement this method, but it's not the only way. You can just as well create a separate context manager class (one with `__enter__` and `__exit__` methods) and return an instance of that class from the method. I appreciate the concern for helping people get this right, but I think right now this documentation is a bit too verbose; goes a little too far in documenting the behavior of `contextlib.contextmanager`, which is out of MonkeyType's scope. I would defer to the Python documentation when we can, rely heavily on the example code (which is the first thing most people will look at anyway) and revise to something shorter:

"This method must return a [context manager](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#with-statement-context-managers) instance. In most cases the simplest way to do this will be via the [contextlib.contextmanager](https://docs.python.org/3/library/contextlib.html#contextlib.contextmanager) decorator. For example, if you run MonkeyType against a Django codebase, you can setup Django before the command runs::"
okay

This is wrong if the assumption is that `v` can be something else than a string. If `v` can only be a string, then the `elif` is redundant.

The reason this code is wrong for non-strings is that `v` can be falsey (e.g. `0`, `False`, `None`, `[]`, etc.) and such case `encoded_val` is undefined (or takes the value of the previous iteration of this loop):

```
In [32]: all_params = { 'CallUUID': 'fake-call-uuid', 'Duration': 0, 'CallStatus': 'ringing' }

In [33]: all_params.items()
Out[33]: [('Duration', 0), ('CallUUID', 'fake-call-uuid'), ('CallStatus', 'ringing')]

In [34]: encoded_request = 'https://myserver.com/plivo_call/'

In [35]: for k,v in all_params.items():
    ...:     encoded_key = k.encode('utf-8')
    ...:     if isinstance(v, basestring):
    ...:         encoded_val = v
    ...:     elif v:
    ...:         encoded_val = str(v)
    ...:     encoded_request += encoded_key + encoded_val
    ...:
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
NameError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-35-d5ef27fce03f> in <module>()
      5     elif v:
      6         encoded_val = str(v)
----> 7     encoded_request += encoded_key + encoded_val
      8

NameError: name 'encoded_val' is not defined
```

Why is this version 2? And I'd recommend just having a constant with the current version. The only place an instance with an older version will exist is in a serialized state and when you deserialize it you update it right away to the latest version. So you have to store the version when serializing but no need to store it as a member variable on the class when it can just be a constant.

```
record_dict = dict((k,record[k]) for k in record.dtype.names)

```

The user cannot choose the `nonce` value, if he does one of the following will happen:

- He will choose a `nonce` that has been used by the health check, in this case he will get a false acknowledged confirmation (because the message was acknowledged in the past)
- He will choose a new `nonce` and then the health check will get a false acknowledgment.

Either way is incorrect.

The only thing we actually need to do here is stuff the incoming chain onto `se.incomingChains`. However, since that chain is unbuffered, this can block if no workers are alive to receive the incoming chain (i.e. they just exited because they saw an error.)

The second arm of the select aborts the write to `se.incomingChains` if the step executor is shutting down, since it doesn't make sense to execute any more steps anyway.

Yeah, this is a bit awkward, but it has to be this way for now while we're mixing C structs and C++ objects. When we `malloc` the `LocalActorInfo`, it might get initialized with garbage values. A `std::unique_ptr` that is a member of the struct will also get initialized with garbage values, and it will seem to hold a pointer, but the pointer isn't valid. When we reset the `std::unique_ptr` to point to the actual object we want it to point to, it tries to clean up the invalid pointer under the hood. So, I used `memset` so that it gets initialized to hold a NULL pointer.
